stories.headline: Lynn Bartels, celebrated Colorado political reporter, dies at 69
stories.summary:
Lynn Bartels, a veteran political reporter widely regarded as the defining voice of Colorado political journalism for three decades, died at age 69 after being diagnosed with brain cancer earlier this year. Her family announced her death on social media, saying they had been "overwhelmed by the love" shared by the public since her diagnosis.

Bartels began her career in Colorado in 1993 as a night cops reporter for the Rocky Mountain News, where she worked until the paper closed in 2009. The Denver Post then hired her, and she remained there until 2015 before transitioning to communications work for then-Secretary of State Wayne Williams.

Colleagues and public figures from across the political spectrum remembered her as a dogged, fair and humorous reporter who held people in power to account regardless of party. 9News journalist Kyle Clark wrote that "no journalist in the last 30 years did a better job covering Colorado politics with such grit, tirelessness, and humor." Former Republican Sen. Cory Gardner called her "the Grand Dame of Colorado politics and political reporting."

Bartels' family said a memorial service is being planned to honor her legacy. She is remembered not only for breaking stories that shaped Colorado political history but for the relationships she built with sources and colleagues throughout her career.

stories.headline: Outside groups spend $1.2 million backing DeGette as June 30 Democratic primary nears
stories.summary:
Three outside political action committees have spent a combined $1.2 million this month to support U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette or oppose her primary challenger Melat Kiros in Colorado's 1st Congressional District race, with most of the spending — $860,000 — concentrated in just the final days before the June 30 primary. DeGette, who has represented the Denver-centered district for nearly 30 years, also leads Kiros in direct fundraising, raising $445,000 from April through early June and entering the final stretch with roughly $460,000 cash on hand.

The largest outside contribution came from Pro-Choice Majority Action, which spent nearly $460,000 on TV ads supporting DeGette. A group called Mile High Accountability Project spent $350,000 on digital advertising in her support, while Project 218 spent $400,000 opposing Kiros. On the other side, Justice Democrats spent nearly $178,000 supporting Kiros, who also received a formal endorsement from U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders on Friday.

In other key Democratic primary races, U.S. Sen. John Hickenlooper led primary challenger Julie Gonzales in fundraising, and Manny Rutinel led Shannon Bird in the 8th Congressional District race. All three primaries are set for June 30.

Kiros, an attorney who identifies as a democratic socialist, and University of Colorado Regent Wanda James are also on the ballot challenging DeGette. The race has drawn national attention and significant outside spending as progressive groups test the strength of the incumbent against a left-wing challenge.

stories.headline: Man arrested on suspicion of first-degree murder in June 7 Denver shooting near South Park Hill
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested 35-year-old Joseph York on Thursday on suspicion of first-degree murder in connection with the fatal shooting of 25-year-old Elijah Barr near East Colfax Avenue and North Dahlia Street, in the area between the South Park Hill and Hale neighborhoods. Barr was found with multiple gunshot wounds in the early hours of June 7 and was pronounced dead at the hospital.

Detectives identified York as a suspect through witness interviews and surveillance video, according to a Denver Police Department news release. Investigators believe York and Barr were arguing before the shooting occurred.

York is being held without bail at the Downtown Detention Center and was scheduled to appear in Denver County Court on Saturday. The arrest comes nearly two weeks after the shooting.

stories.headline: Woman sentenced to 6 years in prison for DUI crash on Colorado 93 that killed Littleton woman
stories.summary:
A Jefferson County District Court judge sentenced 20-year-old Sage Valentine to six years in prison this week for a drunken driving crash on Colorado Highway 93 that killed 64-year-old Cynthia "Cindy" Myers of Littleton. Valentine had pleaded guilty in March to two counts of vehicular homicide related to reckless driving and DUI.

The crash occurred at approximately 6 a.m. on February 20, 2025, when Valentine, driving 60 mph southbound near mile marker 2 north of Golden, crossed into oncoming traffic and struck two vehicles before hitting Myers' car head-on. Colorado State Patrol investigators found evidence that Valentine had been drinking in Boulder the night before and had not slept before driving to work that morning.

Myers' husband, Greg Myers, spoke at the sentencing hearing and said his wife was months away from retirement when she was killed. As part of the plea agreement, prosecutors dismissed one misdemeanor DUI count, and Valentine also pleaded guilty to a traffic infraction for driving without insurance.

stories.headline: WestJet flight evacuated at DIA after blown tires shred landing gear before takeoff
stories.summary:
A WestJet flight bound for Calgary was evacuated at Denver International Airport on Thursday afternoon after an incident just before takeoff left two landing gear tires shredded. The airport confirmed the evacuation, and passengers were removed from the aircraft on the runway.

The incident occurred as the aircraft was preparing to depart, prompting emergency procedures. DIA officials and WestJet have not yet released details on what caused the tires to fail or whether any passengers or crew were injured.

The flight disruption added to travel complications at DIA and is under review by airport and airline officials.

stories.headline: Aurora City Council set to finalize votes on police oversight office, tax increase ballot measures
stories.summary:
Aurora City Council members are scheduled to cast final votes Monday on a package of significant ordinances, including the creation of an independent Office of Public Safety Accountability to oversee the Aurora Police Department, measures to put tax increases on the November ballot, and new inspection requirements for detention facilities in the city.

The Office of Public Safety Accountability, funded with $330,000, will operate independently under the city manager and have unrestricted access to police records, body camera footage, facilities and personnel. Aurora police will be required to notify the office of any critical incident within 30 minutes. The office was initiated as part of Aurora's consent decree with the Colorado Attorney General's Office, which is expected to expire in February 2027.

If voters approve the proposed sales tax increase — the city's first since 1993 — Aurora's sales and use tax would rise by 0.325 percentage points, with revenue directed toward the "Build Up Aurora" infrastructure program covering road construction, police and fire station upgrades, and transportation safety improvements. Two additional ordinances requiring periodic inspections and disease reporting from detention facilities also await final approval after initially passing with some opposition from conservative council members.

stories.headline: PNC Bank completes rebranding of former FirstBank branches across Denver metro
stories.summary:
PNC Bank has completed its rebranding of former FirstBank locations across the Denver metro area, with new signage now going up at branch locations including the prominent corner of Speer Boulevard and Broadway in Denver. The transition marks the final visible step in PNC's acquisition and integration of FirstBank's Colorado operations.

Separately, Karen Gerwitz announced she is stepping down this fall after 16 years leading World Trade Center Denver, citing work-life balance. The organization has begun a search for a new CEO to lead the trade promotion and business development nonprofit.

The dual leadership and branding changes reflect ongoing consolidation and transition in Denver's business and financial services sector.

stories.headline: Denver-area space companies compete for contracts as NASA's lunar economy gains momentum
stories.summary:
Denver-area aerospace and technology companies are positioning themselves to capture contracts from NASA's growing lunar economy, driven by renewed momentum behind the Artemis program and its goal of establishing a permanent base on the moon. Multiple Colorado firms are vying for roles ranging from hardware and propulsion to logistics and communications infrastructure.

Colorado has long been a hub for the space industry, home to major operations for Lockheed Martin, United Launch Alliance and a growing constellation of space startups. The potential for sustained lunar missions and eventually a moon base is seen as a generational opportunity for the region's aerospace sector.

Industry observers note that federal investment in the Artemis program, combined with commercial partnerships, is helping Colorado companies build the technical and contractual groundwork needed to compete for long-duration lunar economy work over the coming decade.

stories.headline: Broncos OLB Jonathon Cooper absent from minicamp amid domestic violence charges
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos outside linebacker Jonathon Cooper has been absent from the team's mandatory minicamp as he faces domestic violence charges, according to reports from NFL trade and roster tracking outlets. Head coach Sean Payton acknowledged the situation and indicated he feels the league has addressed the matter.

Cooper's absence adds a roster complication for the Broncos as they head into the offseason following minicamp. The team wrapped its offseason program this week, with Payton ending minicamp a day early ahead of the upcoming facility move.

The league's personal conduct policy governs how teams and players handle such situations during the offseason and into training camp, which opens in late July.

stories.headline: Nuggets hold 26th pick as NBA Draft approaches; Peyton Watson free agency looms large
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets hold the 26th overall pick as the NBA Draft approaches, with mock drafts offering varied predictions on who the team might select. The draft comes as Denver faces a significant offseason decision on restricted free agent Peyton Watson, whose rising stock around the league puts him in line for a substantial contract offer.

The Nuggets are already navigating a roster in transition after losing multiple starters, and Watson's potential departure would deepen the team's depth concerns heading into next season. Reports indicate Denver's front office is weighing trade options and roster moves to address the team's needs alongside its draft selection.

Denver's most recent postseason included a Game 6 win over the Oklahoma City Thunder in the Western Conference Semifinals. The team's offseason moves, including the draft and Watson's free agency situation, will shape its roster construction heading into the 2026-27 campaign.

stories.headline: Rockies host Pirates in game 2 of series at Coors Field
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies (29-47) hosted the Pittsburgh Pirates (38-38) in game 2 of their series at Coors Field on Saturday evening. The Rockies remain in last place in the NL West, while the Pirates sit fourth in the NL Central at the .500 mark.

Colorado's record is among the worst in the National League as the team continues a difficult 2026 season. The series against Pittsburgh represents a matchup of two clubs on opposite ends of the competitive spectrum within their respective divisions.

stories.headline: Denver weekend weather: Hot and sunny Saturday, afternoon thunderstorms possible through weekend
stories.summary:
Denver and the Front Range are heading into a hot weekend, with temperatures climbing into the low 90s on Saturday before a chance of afternoon and evening thunderstorms. An Ozone Action Day Alert was issued Friday by the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment for the Front Range Urban Corridor, warning that hot, stagnant air could create poor air quality conditions affecting people with respiratory issues.

The National Weather Service in Boulder said elevated fire weather conditions are expected in the mountains and valleys Friday afternoon, with isolated strong storms possible along the Palmer Divide. Wind gusts of up to 18 mph were forecast for Friday, with overnight lows around 58 degrees.

The hot and dry stretch continues a pattern of elevated fire danger across the region. Residents are advised to monitor air quality alerts and be prepared for fast-developing afternoon thunderstorms through the weekend.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I retired from comedy. Then I came back. Turns out retirement doesn't have an audience.
